Today independent Office Actuary released projected national health expenditures 2017-2026.
National health expenditure growth expected average percent annually over 2017-2026, according report published today "Ahead Print" Health Affairs authored Office Actuary Centers Medicare Medicaid Services (CMS).
Growth national health spending projected faster than projected growth Gross Domestic Product (GDP) percentage point over 2017-2026.
result, report projects health share rise from 17.9 percent 2016 19.7 percent 2026.
outlook national health spending enrollment over next decade expected driven primarily fundamental economic demographic factors: trends disposable personal income, increases prices medical goods services, shifts enrollment from private health insurance Medicare that result from continued aging baby-boom generation into Medicare eligibility.
"Personal healthcare spending" measures spending medical goods services provided directly patients.
Over projection period, growth personal healthcare prices growth intensity care provided collectively explain about three quarters growth personal healthcare spending.
report also found that 2026, federal, state local governments projected finance percent national health spending, from percent 2016.
Under current law, national health spending projected grow average rate percent year 2017-26 reach $5.7 trillion 2026.
While this projected average annual growth rate more modest than that percent observed over longer-term history prior recession (1990-2007), more rapid than been experienced 2008-16 (4.2 percent).
Health spending projected grow percentage point faster than Gross Domestic Product (GDP) year over 2017-26 period; result, health share expected rise from
Projected national health spending enrollment growth over next decade largely driven fundamental economic demographic factors: changes projected income growth, increases prices medical goods services, enrollment shifts from private health insurance Medicare related aging population.
Among major payers health care, growth spending Medicare (7.4 percent year) Medicaid (5.8 percent year) both substantial contributors rate national health expenditure growth projection period.

recent enactment legislation that eliminated individual mandate expected lead reduction insured rates.

2026, national health expenditures share expected rise 19.7 percent almost one-fifth GDP.

design nation's health care financing system even more crucial than amount spending.
Because high costs health care, great variability individual health care needs, unequal distribution income wealth, health care system that takes care everyone requires egalitarian transfer from healthy wealthy sick poor.
well designed single payer national health program does precisely that, requires implementation effective public (government) program.
private sector accomplish transfer effective equitable manner?
Absolutely not, have century experience with private sector prove this.
Even with high level government involvement that have (the government funds about two-thirds health care) design health care financing system failed implement tools that would increase effectiveness efficiency allocation health care funds resources.

press release quotes Administrator Seema Verma: "Today's report from independent Office Actuary shows that healthcare spending expected continue growing more quickly than rest economy.
This another call action increase market competition consumer choice within programs help control costs ensure that programs available future generations."
government turn over private sector recipe disaster policies already into place current administration previous administrations demonstrating, they promise much more.

Loukas Karabarbounis Associate Professor Economics University Minnesota, faculty research fellow National Bureau Economic Research, associate editor Journal Monetary Economics.
earned Economics from Harvard University 2010.
recipient 2016 Sloan Research Fellowship awarded Alfred Sloan Foundation.
latest research focuses topics such global decline labor's share income, productivity capital flows southern Europe, cyclical fluctuations labor markets.
